title: ASU Tutoring
summary: Tutoring is one of the fastest ways to turn confusion into a plan. You do not need to be failing to use it.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Go to a tutoring space, check in, and work through the class material with a peer tutor.

steps.title: Find your subject and campus option
steps.description: Start on the ASU tutoring site and look for the course or support area that matches your class.
bullets: Open tutoring.asu.edu and choose the tutoring menu.
Look for your subject, writing support, or the campus tutoring schedule.
Check whether your class is listed as drop-in, appointment-based, or both.
steps.reassurance: A lot of students use tutoring during the first rough week, especially before they know how hard a class will feel.

visual.title: Tutoring site and schedule
visual.caption: You are usually looking for your course, campus, and whether it is drop-in or scheduled.

steps.title: Walk in and check in
steps.description: Once you get there, the first step is usually just checking in with your name, course, and what you need help with.
bullets: Bring your ASU ID, assignment, notes, or quiz review sheet.
Tell the front desk or tutor which class you are in and what is confusing.
Sit down with the tutor and show the exact problem that is blocking you.
steps.reassurance: You do not need a perfect explanation. Saying “I do not know where I got lost” is enough to start.

visual.title: Check-in desk and study tables
visual.caption: Most tutoring spaces feel more like a study room than a high-pressure appointment.

steps.title: Leave with one clear next step
steps.description: The best tutoring session ends with a next step you can do on your own after you leave.
bullets: Ask the tutor to show one example all the way through.
Write down the pattern or mistake you need to watch for.
If you still feel stuck, ask when the next tutoring session is.

visual.title: A smaller problem than when you walked in
visual.caption: You are not supposed to master the whole course in one visit. You are trying to get unstuck.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: Use the tutoring site to find an online option, then join from your laptop with your class materials ready.

steps.title: Pick an online tutoring option
steps.description: Use the tutoring schedule to find a virtual session for your subject or support area.
bullets: Open the tutoring schedule and filter for online help if needed.
Confirm the time, platform, and whether you need to register first.
Keep your course name and assignment title handy before you join.

visual.title: Laptop, assignment, and ASURITE sign-in
visual.caption: The hardest part is usually just joining on time with the right file open.

steps.title: Join and explain the exact sticking point
steps.description: The tutor can help faster if you show the exact question, draft, or concept that is blocking you.
bullets: Open your notes, textbook page, or assignment before the call starts.
Use the chat or voice to say where you got lost.
Share your screen if the tutor asks to see the problem.
steps.reassurance: Lots of students feel awkward the first time. The tutor is used to people joining and saying, “I honestly do not know what I am doing.”

visual.title: Short, focused help
visual.caption: You are allowed to ask one small question first instead of telling the whole story perfectly.

steps.title: Save the next time you could come back
steps.description: Before you leave, make it easy to return if you need a second session.
bullets: Bookmark the tutoring page or schedule.
Write down the tutor's advice for what to practice next.
Set a reminder if you want to come back before the next quiz.

visual.title: Make the second visit easier than the first
visual.caption: The first visit is usually the hardest one.

modes.label: Drop-in
modes.summary: Show up during open tutoring hours with your course materials and ask for help without scheduling ahead.

steps.title: Confirm that your class has drop-in support
steps.description: Some tutoring areas are true walk-in hours and some require appointments, so check before you go.
bullets: Look at the tutoring site for your subject's drop-in times.
Double-check the campus location or virtual drop-in link.
Bring the worksheet, notes, or review guide you want to use.

visual.title: Show up without a long setup process
visual.caption: Drop-in is usually the easiest first try if making appointments feels like a barrier.

steps.title: Tell them what you came in for
steps.description: When you arrive, say the course and the one thing you most want help with today.
bullets: Start with the course code or subject area.
Point to the question or concept that is giving you trouble.
Ask if there is time to stay for a full explanation or if you should return later.
steps.reassurance: You are not interrupting anyone by showing up during drop-in hours. That is exactly what those hours are for.

visual.title: A one-sentence explanation is enough
visual.caption: Try: “I am in MAT 265 and I keep missing the same kind of derivative problem.”

steps.title: Decide whether to come back or switch resources
steps.description: After the session, decide whether you need another tutoring visit, office hours, or a study group next.
bullets: Ask if there is another tutoring block later this week.
Note whether office hours would help with the same question.
Set one follow-up action before you leave the building.

visual.title: Use tutoring as one tool, not the only tool
visual.caption: Good help usually points you toward the next useful stop too.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I am using tutoring for the first time and I want to make sure I am focusing on the right problem.
questionSet.defaultProTip: If you have a bad quiz, bring that exact quiz or review sheet. Concrete materials make the session much better.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: This can feel more awkward in your head than it does in the room. The tutor expects students to arrive confused.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: I got a low grade on my first quiz and I do not understand what I am missing.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: I am working extra hours and I am worried I am falling behind in one class.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: I feel behind in multiple classes and I do not know where to start catching up.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: I can make time for tutoring, but I need help figuring out which class needs it first.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I am trying to use tutoring well, but I am not sure what to ask for.

first-year: What is the best way to use tutoring for this class each week?
Can you help me figure out the first concept I need to fix before the next quiz?
What should I bring next time so the session goes faster?
How do I know whether I should also go to office hours for this question?
What is one thing I can practice on my own after this session?
second-year: Which topic is hurting me the most right now in this class?
Can you help me make a short study plan before the next exam?
What mistakes do students usually make on problems like this?
Would you recommend tutoring again this week or a different resource next?
What should I review before I come back?
third-year: What should I focus on first if I am balancing a heavy course load?
Can you help me identify whether this is a concept problem or a time-management problem?
What would make my questions more useful when I come back?
Is there a pattern in the mistakes I am making that I am not seeing?
What should I ask my professor or TA after this?
fourth-year-plus: Can you help me target the specific topic that is putting this course at risk?
What is the fastest high-impact way to prepare before the next graded assignment?
If I have limited time this week, where should I focus my effort first?
Would you recommend tutoring, office hours, or both for this issue?
What should I do if I still feel stuck after trying these steps?
